sales.title: Trajan (AD 98-117). AE sestertius (32mm, 25.68 gm, 7h). NGC AU 5/5 - 2/5. Rome, AD 114-117. IMP CAES NER TRAIANO OPTIMO AVG GER DAC P M TR P COS VI P P, laureate, draped bust of Trajan right, seen from behind / SENATVS POPVLVSQVE ROMANVS, Fortuna seated left, feet on stool, grounded rudder in outstretched right hand, cornucopia cradled in left arm; FORT RED / S C in exergue. RIC II 652.  Marcus Ulpius Trajanus, better known as Trajan, carved his name in history as one of ancient Rome's most illustrious emperors, renowned for his military prowess and strategic brilliance. Born the son of a prominent Roman senator under the reign of Vespasian, Trajan followed his father's career in provincial military administration. Having just received the title Germanicus from his exploits at the Germanic frontier, Trajan ascended to power in AD 98 with the death of the elderly Emperor Nerva. Under Trajan the Roman Empire reached its greatest territorial extent, encompassing vast swathes of land across Europe, Asia, and Africa. His military campaigns, particularly the Dacian Wars, secured valuable resources and strategic advantages for the empire. Trajan's conquests also extended into the Middle East, where he captured territories such as Armenia and Mesopotamia, further consolidating Roman influence in the region. Beyond his military achievements, Trajan was renowned for his benevolent governance and commitment to public welfare. He implemented extensive building projects, including the construction of roads, bridges, and aqueducts, which facilitated trade and communication throughout the empire, while also completing iconic landmarks such as Trajan's Column and Trajan's Forum in Rome, showcasing his enduring legacy as a patron of architecture and urban development. Trajan's reign is remembered as achieving the heights of the golden age of the Roman Empire, characterized by stability, prosperity, and imperial expansion. The Senate conferred onto him the title of "Best" (Optimus) and future emperors would be inaugurated with the phrase that they be "luckier than Augustus, better than Trajan" (Felicior Augusto, melior Traiano).   HID05401242017  © 2026 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice
sales.description: Heritage Auctions, Inc. | ['Auction #61605']

sales.title: Trajan Decius (AD 249-251). AR antoninianus (22mm, 5h). NGC XF. Rome. IMP C M Q TRAIANVS DECIVS AVG, radiate, draped, and cuirassed bust of Trajan Decius right, seen from behind / VICT-ORIA-AVG, Victory advancing left, wreath in outstretched right hand, palm cradled in left arm. RIC V.III 29c.   Gaius Messius Quintus Trajanus Decius or Trajan Decius adopted the name "Trajan" as a call back to Trajan himself. His rule, however, would be anything but like that of Trajan. Marred by revolt, plague, and persecution of Roman Christians, he is notable for his infamy. Decius's most controversial act was that of forceful sacrifice, wherein Christian subjects were put to death if their pagan sacrifice requirement was not met. Christian scholars later attributed the Cyprian Plague of AD 251-266 to this act directly as "divine retribution by God". Decius would ultimately be killed during the battle of Abritus, wherein both him and his son, Herennius Etruscus would fall, being the first Roman emperor to fall in battle to a foreign army. While being quite infamous as a ruler, Decius leaves his numismatic footprint through his mass production of the largest officially minted Roman coin, the Double Sestertius. Furthermore his "Divi Series" saw the restoration of many past Roman emperors dedicated on coinage.   HID05401242017  © 2026 Heritage Auctions | All Rights Reserved www.HA.com/TexasAuctioneerLicenseNotice
